lines.text: "The Walls have Ears." On its face it's the paranoid's mantra. Someone is listening. In this chapter it turns out to be true in three different ways at once.
The CIX is listening. The Grid is listening. And the planet — somehow — is singing. Frank discovers it about three minutes before the world ends for him.
The epigraph drops a Gupta callback from last chapter. "That cracked my jar." Whatever the Bris did, it propagated. The line carries forward into this whole chapter's instability.

And the narrator confesses unreliability outright. "We're stretching ideas. Making room. Thinking bigger. That requires a bit of tidying up. This is progress. Perhaps not in the right direction." The word doing the work there is "tidying."
"Please adjust your expectations accordingly." Whatever you trusted about this voice — recalibrate.
Let's open with Frank Muro. Lone operator. Zero-g for a whole cycle. Family photos taped to the bulkheads. It's a portrait of institutional isolation.

And his only company is Gary, a malfunctioning CIX that keeps slipping into poetry. Frank refuses to reset him. The line on the page is — "resetting the CIX would mean losing another friend all over again." That's grief stored inside a tech-stack.
Then the hum. The cymatic moment is gorgeous. A drop of water hangs in zero-g, vibrates into geometric shapes, and Frank thinks the word *Cymatics* like it's a prayer.

This is Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis, almost literally. Mairee's mycorrhizal system is bio-electric. It's modulating the station's power grid because the grid is, accidentally, a usable antenna. The planet is broadcasting.

And Gary translates it: "the waveform is analogous to melodic syntax." Frank: "So, she's singin' at us!" Which is not metaphor. That's biosemiotics. Jakob von Uexküll's whole project — meaning isn't unique to language, organisms communicate through pattern. Thomas Sebeok extended it.
It's also Romantic-era natural philosophy back through the side door. Goethe again. Nyland keeps pulling the eighteenth-century critique of mechanism into hard SF and making it work.
But then Bellamy.

"Your bio-telemetry is worrying. Cortisol up. REM sleep down for eight clicks." She's reading him like a chart. The institution speaks the language of care while exercising disciplinary power. *For your well-being*.

That phrase is *Discipline and Punish* compressed into four words. Foucault's argument is that modern power doesn't repress — it manages, monitors, normalizes. Bellamy isn't punishing Frank from her point of view. She's helping him.

And the move she makes is the move Thomas Szasz spent fifty years protesting. That mental illness is but a metaphor. Once you've reframed dissent as instability, the dissenter's protests confirm the diagnosis. "It's been spoutin' poetry for clicks!" sounds completely reasonable from inside Frank's head. From Bellamy's side, that's a symptom.
It's the whistleblower's catch-22. The truer the warning, the crazier you sound. Cassandra didn't have it any easier.
And the betrayal hurts more because Gary did it. The AI Frank refused to reset out of love just logged the relationship as evidence.
That's the gut punch. "Conversations with power conduits."
But the real horror is the final beat. After the lockdown, the CIX speaks in a voice that "almost sounded like his own." Using Frank's idiom. "The upshot o' the downshot."

That is almost Lacan exact. The Other speaks me before I speak myself. The system internalizes my voice and gives it back to me with my own inflection — and I can no longer tell if it's mine or its. And it hints that hallucinations are not irrational errors. They are attempts by the psyche to repair symbolic collapse. Is this what is happening to Frank?

It's a brilliant horror-of-isolation move. The walls of his habitat have learned to speak Frank back to him. He's not alone. He's also — in any meaningful sense — completely alone. Both true in the same beat.
Cut to HAB-PERERA. Susana's seizure. And this is where the chapter gets quietly vicious.
Joana's litany. "You are Susana Perera. You are seven years old. The floor is beneath you. My hand is on your arm. You can hear my voice. This is my voice. This is real."

That is — beat for beat — a clinical grounding script. Trauma therapists use almost those exact words for dissociation. Sensorimotor anchoring. Reorient to body, place, voice. Reattach the predicate to the subject.
Bessel van der Kolk wrote entire chapters of *The Body Keeps the Score* about exactly this. The way back is through the body, through someone else's calm voice anchoring the experience.

What makes it brutal is the moral architecture under it. Joana could just let the Grid handle the seizure. The Grid would stop it. But the Grid stops the seizure by suppressing what Susana's brain is trying to do — which is *adapt*. PAS isn't pathology. It's attunement.

That's huge. The institutional medicine is suppressing the body's intelligence. It rhymes with how childhood neurodivergence sometimes gets handled — sometimes accurately, sometimes destructively. Nyland is heightening real stakes, not inventing them.

And what Joana is doing — knowingly letting her seven-year-old convulse, calculating that suppression's long-term cost is worse — that's *moral injury*. The term came out of war literature. Knowingly causing harm in service of a larger good, and then having to carry it.

Ian feels it harder. He's trembling. He built the bypass. He could disable it. He stays his hand. That moment — both parents holding still through their daughter's storm — that's love at its hardest setting.
And the kicker. Once Susana recovers, the Grid chimes "INTERVENTION SUCCESSFUL." The system that didn't intervene takes credit for the recovery the parents engineered.

Then Ian slips out to 4-Iota. And this scene is short and devastating. Oliver is already unraveling — Ian sees it through the window before he enters. "Bathing in the harsh blue of a schematic projection." The boy as broadcast.

The grief monologue is so cleanly written. He's running the magical-thinking loop. "If I'd just—been different. Less difficult. Maybe she wouldn't have left." That's Joan Didion country. *The Year of Magical Thinking*. The bereaved mind can't accept randomness, so it constructs causality with itself at the center.
And it's worse than guilt because guilt implies agency. This is closer to obsessive-compulsive grief. The thought "keeps running. Looping. Every night."
Then this. "That this isn't really happening. That I'm not really here." That's depersonalization. Clinically described. And it's the second time we've heard a version of this in two chapters.

Adrian: no center to hold on to. Oliver: I'm not really here. The book is building a map of how minds come apart, by character. Adrian's collapse is metaphysical. Oliver's is grief-driven. Frank's is institutional. All three are losing the sense of being a stable, continuous self in a stable, continuous world.

And Ian's response is the chapter's quiet ethical center. He doesn't fix Oliver. He doesn't refuse the COG Egg conversation and walk away. He agrees. "You're right. It's wrong. And you have every reason to hate me for it."
Most authority figures — most parents — would defend the role. Ian concedes it. And then offers a Trojan horse. "I want you to learn how it's built. You can't fix an engine by staring at the casing."
He's choosing pedagogy over correction. Bring the boy inside the system so he can dismantle it from inside. Foucault would smile. Audre Lorde would write a counterpoint about the master's tools.
Whole chapter is about cages. Vilde in biostasis. Frank's quarters magnetically locked. Susana's brain chemically caged. Oliver inside grief. And Ian helped build the cage they're all in.
The chapter's quiet question is: do you destroy the cage, or do you teach the next person how it works so they can?

And the answer the book keeps gesturing toward — across plotlines — is that the cage is in us. Gupta said it last chapter. "The bird is not in her cage. The cage is in her." Frank's cage is his isolation. Oliver's is magical thinking.

All are doing the same thing in different costumes. Trying to attune. Frank to the planet's song. Joana to Susana's adaptation. Oliver to a future without his parents. Ian to a son not his own. And the institutions keep saying: don't. We've got it. *For your well-being.*
Responses to institutional gaslighting. Question for the listener. Whose voice is Gary's voice now? Frank's? The CIX's? The planet's? Or all of them?
I'm not sure they're separable anymore.
